title: The main provisions of Regulation (EU) No. 536/2014 include:(with note, foot-note, links)

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: The main provisions of Regulation (EU) No. 536/2014 include:(with note, foot-note, links)

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Regulation (EU) No. 536/2014 lays down the rules for the conduct of clinical trials of medicinal products for human use. The main provisions of the regulation include:

1. Simplification and Streamlining of Clinical Trial Application Procedures: The regulation introduces a harmonized procedure for submitting and evaluating clinical trial applications across all member states of the European Union (EU). The application process will be centralized through a new EU portal and database, allowing for faster approval times and reduced administrative burdens.

2. Improved Patient Safety and Protection: The regulation includes increased requirements for informed consent and transparency in the conduct of clinical trials. There will be greater scrutiny of trials involving vulnerable populations, such as children or patients with mental disorders, and stricter rules for the use of placebos.

3. Strengthened Pharmacovigilance: The regulation requires continuous monitoring of the safety and efficacy of medicinal products throughout their lifecycle. This includes the establishment of a new EU pharmacovigilance database, which will allow for better tracking and analysis of adverse reactions.

4. Increased Transparency and Access to Clinical Trial Data: The regulation mandates the publication of summaries of clinical trial results in publicly accessible databases, regardless of whether the results are positive or negative. This will enhance the transparency of clinical research and help to identify potential safety issues or unmet medical needs.

5. Enhanced Cooperation and Information Sharing: The regulation requires greater collaboration between the EU member states and the European Medicines Agency (EMA), as well as with other international regulatory authorities. This will facilitate the exchange of information and best practices, and improve the consistency and efficiency of regulatory decision-making.

Overall, Regulation (EU) No. 536/2014 represents a significant step forward in the regulation of clinical trials within the EU, improving patient safety and protection, increasing transparency and access to clinical trial data, and enhancing cooperation and information sharing among regulatory authorities.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Provide a list of literature and references, notes, foot-notes, links on the topic "INTERNATIONAL-LEGAL MECHANISMS OF ENSURING THE SAFETY OF MEDICINE TRAFFIC"

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
1. World Health Organization. (2020). International legal frameworks for public health: A brief overview. https://www.who.int/health-topics/international-health-regulations/international-legal-frameworks-for-public-health-a-brief-overview
- This resource provides a brief overview of the international legal frameworks that exist to protect public health, including the International Health Regulations (IHR) and other relevant conventions.

2. United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2018). International drug control conventions. https://www.unodc.org/unodc/en/drug-trafficking/international-drug-control-conventions.html
- This resource provides information on the three main international drug control conventions, which aim to ensure the availability of controlled substances for medical and scientific purposes while preventing their diversion and misuse.

3. European Medicines Agency. (2018). International cooperation. https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/about-us/international-cooperation
- This resource provides information on the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) international cooperation activities, which include collaborating with regulatory authorities in other countries to ensure the safety and efficacy of medicines.

4. World Trade Organization. (1994). Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights. https://www.wto.org/english/docs_e/legal_e/27-trips_01_e.htm
- This resource provides information on the TRIPS Agreement, which sets out minimum standards for the protection and enforcement of intellectual property rights, including those related to pharmaceuticals.

5. Gupta, U. C., & Gupta, S. C. (2020). International drug regulatory affairs: A primer. Indian Journal of Pharmacology, 52(1), 7–12. https://doi.org/10.4103/ijp.IJP_42_20
- This article provides an overview of the international regulatory landscape for drugs, including the role of the World Health Organization, the International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use, and other relevant organizations.

6. World Medical Association. (2013). Declaration of Helsinki - Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Subjects. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki-ethical-principles-for-medical-research-involving-human-subjects/
- This resource provides information on the Declaration of Helsinki, which sets out ethical principles for the conduct of medical research involving human subjects and is recognized as a cornerstone of research ethics.

7. United Nations. (1961). Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs. https://www.unodc.org/pdf/convention_1961_en.pdf
- This resource provides information on the Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, which seeks to prevent the illicit production and trafficking of narcotic drugs while ensuring their availability for medical and scientific purposes.

8. International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use. (2016). ICH Good Clinical Practice. https://database.ich.org/sites/default/files/ICH%20E6%20Guideline.pdf
- This resource provides information on the ICH Good Clinical Practice guidelines, which provide a framework for the design, conduct, and reporting of clinical trials and are widely recognized as an international standard for clinical trial quality.

Note: This is not an exhaustive list and other relevant literature and resources may be available.
